passages.title: How Seeds Travel
passages.body: Plants cannot walk, but their seeds can still move far from the parent plant. Some seeds are light and have thin wings or fluffy hairs that catch the wind, floating them to new soil. Maple seeds spin like tiny helicopters as they fall. Other seeds ride inside sweet fruits; animals eat the fruit and later deposit the seeds in new places. Still others have tiny hooks that cling to fur or socks. Each strategy helps a young plant find space, sunlight, and water without competing too closely with its parent.

passages.title: Libraries Through Time
passages.body: Long ago, libraries were rare collections of scrolls or handwritten books, sometimes chained to desks. Only wealthy institutions or rulers could afford large collections. After the printing press spread in Europe, books became cheaper to produce, and public reading rooms slowly opened. Today, a library might lend paper books, e-books, audiobooks, and films, and offer computers and quiet study space. Librarians help people find trustworthy information—a skill that matters for homework and for everyday decisions.

passages.title: Earth's Water Cycle
passages.body: Energy from the sun warms lakes, rivers, and oceans. Some liquid water evaporates into invisible water vapour that rises into the air. As the vapour cools high in the sky, it condenses into tiny droplets that form clouds. When droplets combine and grow heavy, they fall as rain or snow—called precipitation. Much of that water soaks into soil, runs into streams, or returns to the sea, and the cycle continues. The total amount of water on Earth stays about the same; it mainly changes location and state among liquid, gas, and solid.

passages.title: Volcanoes and the Land
passages.body: A volcano forms where melted rock from deep underground can rise toward the surface. When pressure builds, magma may erupt through a vent, sending lava, ash, and gases into the air. Eruptions can be dangerous for nearby communities, but over long periods they also build new rock layers. Weathered volcanic ash can create fertile soil. Scientists use seismographs, satellites, and gas sensors to monitor volcanoes and warn people when activity increases.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Plants spread their seeds in several clever ways so offspring can grow away from the parent.
All seeds must be eaten by animals to grow.
Maple trees are the only plants that use wind.
Seeds always stay within one metre of the parent plant.
questions.answer: Plants spread their seeds in several clever ways so offspring can grow away from the parent.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what is special about maple seeds?
choices: They are always heavier than acorns.
They spin as they fall, which helps them travel on the wind.
They can only grow in deserts.
They do not need soil to sprout.
questions.answer: They spin as they fall, which helps them travel on the wind.

questions.prompt: Why might an animal help a plant spread seeds?
choices: Animals always bury seeds on purpose for farmers.
Fruit has no connection to seed movement.
The animal eats fruit containing seeds and may leave seeds far from the parent.
Only insects, never mammals, spread seeds.
questions.answer: The animal eats fruit containing seeds and may leave seeds far from the parent.

questions.prompt: What does the passage say hooks on some seeds are for?
choices: To dig holes underground automatically.
To make the seed taste sweeter.
To protect the seed from sunlight only.
To cling to fur, feathers, or clothing and hitch a ride.
questions.answer: To cling to fur, feathers, or clothing and hitch a ride.

questions.prompt: In the passage, "competing" with the parent most nearly means —
choices: using the same limited resources such as space, light, or water.
racing in a sports contest
working on a group project
ignoring the parent completely
questions.answer: using the same limited resources such as space, light, or water.
questions.hintContext: If two plants need the same sunlight and water in one spot, they compete for those resources.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why were large libraries uncommon long ago?
choices: Paper had not been invented anywhere.
Librarians refused to work indoors.
They were expensive and usually limited to wealthy institutions or rulers.
The printing press made too many books at once.
questions.answer: They were expensive and usually limited to wealthy institutions or rulers.

questions.prompt: What change did the printing press help bring about?
choices: Libraries removed all paper books.
Books became cheaper to produce and more people could access them.
Scrolls became the only legal format.
Public libraries closed for two centuries.
questions.answer: Books became cheaper to produce and more people could access them.

questions.prompt: Which detail shows what modern libraries may offer?
choices: Only chained manuscripts and no lending.
Services limited to rulers and their families.
Banning all digital materials.
E-books, audiobooks, computers, and quiet study space.
questions.answer: E-books, audiobooks, computers, and quiet study space.

questions.prompt: What role do librarians play, according to the passage?
choices: They help users find trustworthy information.
They only stamp due dates on fiction.
They replace teachers in every subject.
They decide which books must be destroyed.
questions.answer: They help users find trustworthy information.

questions.prompt: Which title best matches the passage?
choices: How to Build a Printing Press at Home
Why Books Are Heavier Than Scrolls
From Rare Collections to Today's Public Libraries
The History of Maple Seeds in Canada
questions.answer: From Rare Collections to Today's Public Libraries

questions.prompt: What causes liquid water on Earth's surface to become water vapour?
choices: Clouds push water downward only.
The sun's energy warms the water and some of it evaporates.
Precipitation happens before evaporation in every cycle.
Soil absorbs all vapour immediately.
questions.answer: The sun's energy warms the water and some of it evaporates.

questions.prompt: In the passage, what is meant by "condenses" in the sky?
choices: Ice always melts into metal.
Oceans disappear overnight.
Rain only forms underground.
Water vapour cools and forms tiny droplets that make clouds.
questions.answer: Water vapour cools and forms tiny droplets that make clouds.

questions.prompt: What is precipitation?
choices: Rain or snow falling from clouds when droplets grow heavy.
Water soaking only into desert sand.
Evaporation from a single leaf only.
The sun heating only solid rock.
questions.answer: Rain or snow falling from clouds when droplets grow heavy.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the total water on Earth —
choices: doubles every winter
exists only as liquid
stays about the same while moving between places and states.
vanishes after each rainfall
questions.answer: stays about the same while moving between places and states.

questions.prompt: What is one path water might take after it falls as rain?
choices: It always stays in the air forever.
It may soak into soil, run into streams, or return to the sea.
It can only freeze into glaciers immediately.
It leaves Earth for outer space.
questions.answer: It may soak into soil, run into streams, or return to the sea.

questions.prompt: What is magma in this passage?
choices: A type of cloud formed by wind
Solid metal used in seismographs
Frozen water inside glaciers only
Melted rock from deep underground that can rise toward the surface.
questions.answer: Melted rock from deep underground that can rise toward the surface.

questions.prompt: Besides danger, what long-term effect of volcanoes does the passage mention?
choices: New rock layers and fertile soil from weathered ash.
Complete removal of all plants forever
Oceans draining into vents
Stopping the water cycle worldwide
questions.answer: New rock layers and fertile soil from weathered ash.

questions.prompt: How do scientists monitor volcanoes, according to the passage?
choices: By guessing from bird behaviour only
Using only paper maps from the 1600s
With seismographs, satellites, and gas sensors.
By listening to whale songs
questions.answer: With seismographs, satellites, and gas sensors.

questions.prompt: What leaves the vent during an eruption?
choices: Only clean drinking water
Lava, ash, and gases.
Solid steel beams
Seeds and pollen only
questions.answer: Lava, ash, and gases.

questions.prompt: The passage is organized mainly to —
choices: argue that volcanoes should never be studied
list recipes using volcanic ash
compare basketball rules to lava flow
describe what volcanoes are, what eruptions do, and how people study them.
questions.answer: describe what volcanoes are, what eruptions do, and how people study them.
